I'd recommend the following to make the code more robust and readable.  The atomic regular expression operations prevent the need for operating on various chunks.  Also, the more important part is the use of PUB rather than PUSH.  PUSH will distribute requests unequally among PULLers while a PUB on bind will distribute equally among all SUBs.

`r = re.compile('^zmq\+(?P<protocol>\w+)://(?P<tld>\w+):(?P<port1>\d+)\+(?P<port2>\d+)$')
tokens = r.match(url).groupdict()

sink = zmq.Context().socket(zmq.PUB)
sink.connect('{protocol}://{tld}:{port1}'.format(**tokens))

sub = zmq.Context().socket(zmq.SUB)
sub.setsockopt_string(zmq.SUBSCRIBE, u'')
sub.connect('{protocol}://{tld}:{port2}'.format(**tokens))
`

* iterate the file during the initialization of data iterator 
* allow users to input the number of the images

This may seem silly, but I'd like to keep quickcache's footprint as small as possible. Since the dependency on `pytz` is really just to get access to a UTC timezone, I suggest just inlining the top of the first example here: https://docs.python.org/2.7/library/datetime.html#tzinfo-objects into a file called `native_utc.py` or something, so it would just contain

```
from datetime import tzinfo, timedelta, datetime

ZERO = timedelta(0)
HOUR = timedelta(hours=1)

# A UTC class.

class UTC(tzinfo):
    """UTC"""

    def utcoffset(self, dt):
        return ZERO

    def tzname(self, dt):
        return "UTC"

    def dst(self, dt):
        return ZERO

utc = UTC()
```

Then, when you need `utc`, in the code you just added, you can import it from that file. This will make it possible for people to use `quickcache` without also using `pytz` or (maybe even more importantly) when pinned to a version of `pytz` other than `2018.3`. It may be a small gain in practice, but it's also a fixed small cost, and I think it's also good practice.

Not sure the probability of choosing a poor hyperplane by sampling only 2 points, but if you sample 10 then it's pretty easy to find a hyperplane that splits the 10 evenly. You sample a random hyperplane, project all 10 points to the randomly chosen hyperplane, then choose a hyperplane perpendicular to this one that passes through the original splitting the 10 projected points evenly. Make sense? Easier to draw on paper then to describe here.

I'd be careful using a fully randomized Las Vegas algorithm (reject if < 80/20 split) since it costs O(n) to verify (where n is the number of points left to split in the subtree). Tornado does not support cancelling futures, so potentially you could be building up a bunch of futures that never resolve. In the case of using a coroutine (and some other mechanisms that I cannot remember off the top of my head), the ioloop itself will add a done callback to the future. This means that the ioloop (which is forever) has a reference to the future via the done callback. When a future is resolved it deletes all the done callbacks, so als deletes the ioloop -> future reference, allowing the future to be collected. If the future does _not_ resolve, it will not be collected.

We have in the past had an issue where we were waiting for a set of possible states in a loop using `until_any`() where each state was represented using a separate future. Each time the state waiting was done, a new set of futures were creates. When any of the states occured, the `until_any()` future would resolve, but not the states that were not reached. Those states were waited upon by the ioloop, so they would only be collected if they resolved. Some of the states happened very rarely, so copies of those futures built up with each iteration of the loop.

A bodgey hackey way of dealing with that was to have 'until_any()' reach into the future's private members and delete the  done  callbacks on the unresolved futures.

We _might_ run into a similar issue with `until_some()`, but since we are likely to use it only for things that are likely to happen within a fixed time and also in 'short-lived' observation scripts, it seemed better not to hack it. Hopefully tornado futures will one day allow themselves to be cancelled or we will switch over to another async framework :)

```
    >>> more_screens = galsim.PhaseScreenList(screens)
    >>> screens.reset()  # This resets both screens and more_screens back to t=0.
    >>> psf = screens.makePSF(...)   # starts at t=0.  
    >>> # Both screens and more_screens have advanced 1 time step.
    >>> psf2 = more_screens.makePSF(...)   # starts at t=exptime
    >>> # This is the same thing that you would have gotten using screens instead of more_screens.
    >>> assert psf != psf2  # Unequal because they come from different time steps.  Not because they are generated by different PhaseScreenLists.
```

There's a potential buffer overflow problem here. We're accepting user data of arbitrary length less than INT_MAX (PyArg_ParseTuple will raise ValueError if the length of a string is greater than INT_MAX). Adding the three string lengths together can result in integer overflow. Using the result to allocate the buffer below could result in a smaller buffer than you need, causing a buffer overflow when you attempt to copy the strings into the buffer. An attacker could use that to crash the process or execute arbitrary code.

A simple solution to this problem is limit the length of each string to some reasonable size, at the very least something less than one third INT_MAX. That avoids the integer overflow here and any buffer overflow below (sizeof(SEC_CHANNEL_BINDINGS) is only a few bytes).

So some of the properties you have in here share names with other EC2 properties, but are actually slightly different.  For example, this is the ec2 base BlockDeviceMapping:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-ec2-blockdev-mapping.html

But what you actually want is the EC2 Launch Template Block Device Mapping:

https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-ec2-launchtemplate-blockdevicemapping.html

They look incredibly similar, but you'll notice that the `DeviceName` property of the latter is not required, while it is required on the former.

This makes me wonder if the docs are buggy, honestly.  In the end, we may want to name these differently.  Maybe `LaunchTemplateBlockDeviceMapping`?

There are a few of these, so you might want to go through those and verify as well (Placement and Monitoring seem like there might be collisions, I haven't gone through them all though).

As well, it might be worth investigating if the cloudformation schema file actually agrees with the docs.  Honestly, the LaunchTemplate docs in Cloudformation are kind of messy - like the ordering of the properties.

`retry_on_peer` should be either `True` or `False` here.  Not `self.parent.HA_SYNC` because the parent in this case is a firewall, and it doesn't really have an appropriate HA_SYNC setting.  HA_SYNC represents if the object is automatically synced to the HA unit or not, but the firewall is the unit in this case, so it doesn't make sense.

I'm thinking set `retry_on_peer` to `False`.  My logic is, the firewalls in an HA pair could have different content packs installed.  You wouldn't want to ask one firewall for predefined info and when that firewall is down you unknowingly get the result from its peer.  That could break things.  By putting `False` here, if the firewall is down and not responding, it will raise an exception which can be handled by the developer, rather than retrying on the peer and getting potentially wrong information.

```py
from tensorboard.plugins import histogram
```

Note that the difference between `otLog<>Plat` and others log macros is intentional.
`otLog<>Plat()` expects a pointer to instance  but other log macros expect a reference.
```
/**
 * @def otLogCritPlat
 *
 * This method generates a log with level critical for the Platform region.
 *
 * @param[in]  aInstance A pointer to the OpenThread instance.
 * @param[in]  aFormat   A pointer to the format string.
 * @param[in]  ...       Arguments for the format specification.
 *
 */

/**
 * @def otLogCritCoap
 *
 * This method generates a log with level critical for the CoAP region.
 *
 * @param[in]  aInstance    A reference to the OpenThread instance.
 * @param[in]  aFormat      A pointer to the format string.
 * @param[in]  ...          Arguments for the format specification.
 *
 */

````

gem does not (to the best of my knowledge) maintain a running list of all available packages in the same way that apt does.

When you run `apt-get update`, it literally pulls down metadata for _every single available package_ on those apt repos, and then uses that when you run `apt-cache search` or `apt-get install`.

I suspect the reason for this behavior is that this is where the multiple sources of truth (the N apt repositories you have added to `sources.list`) is resolved into a single list of packages; it would be too cumbersome to check each separate repo for a package every time you ran an install (especially given the high dependency count of apt packages).

By contrast, `gem install` (and pip and npm) check a single source of truth, and so no complex local cache is required.

I think it's bad practice (and also causes bad things to happen) to update a list that is being iterated on in a for loop. This would be better if it was a queue that was just getting shifted for each callback which would both empty the list after all callbacks were called and would avoid the updating a list while in a for loop.
